icd_category: K50.8 — Crohn disease of both small and large intestine with complications
one_liner: A 31-year-old female with a 10-year history of this condition presents 6 weeks after ileocecal resection with fever, RLQ pain, and feculent drainage from her surgical incision, consistent with an enterocutaneous fistula with associated intra-abdominal abscess.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient is a 31-year-old female with a 10-year history of this condition (ileal-predominant) who underwent ileocecal resection with primary ileocolic anastomosis 6 weeks ago for a fibrotic stricture causing recurrent small bowel obstructions. Her immediate postoperative course was unremarkable and she was discharged on postoperative day 5 tolerating a regular diet. She was doing well until 2 weeks ago when she noticed intermittent low-grade fevers, increasing RLQ pain near her incision, and malaise. Five days ago, she developed a small area of redness and fluctuance along the inferior aspect of her midline incision. Two days ago, this area spontaneously drained a moderate amount of greenish-brown, foul-smelling fluid that she describes as looking like 'liquid stool.' The drainage has continued intermittently, soaking 3-4 gauze pads per day. She has had decreased appetite, mild nausea (no vomiting), and 2-3 loose bowel movements per day (baseline for her). She has lost 5 pounds since discharge. She reports that she stopped taking her azathioprine 2 weeks before surgery per her surgeon's instructions but has not yet restarted it. She is not currently on any biologic therapy.
stage2.pmh: this condition (ileal-predominant, diagnosed at age 21, previously on azathioprine and infliximab which was discontinued 1 year ago due to infusion reactions), iron deficiency anemia (chronic), vitamin B12 deficiency, prior perianal fistula (treated with seton placement, healed)
stage2.surgical_history: Ileocecal resection with ileocolic anastomosis (6 weeks ago), examination under anesthesia with seton placement for perianal fistula (3 years ago, seton removed after healing)
stage2.medications: Azathioprine 150 mg daily (currently held), mesalamine 1.2 g TID, vitamin B12 injections monthly, iron sulfate 325 mg daily, loperamide 2 mg as needed
stage2.family_history: Father with this condition. Maternal grandmother with colon cancer at age 68.
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker (quit 2 years ago, 5 pack-year history — advised that smoking worsens this condition). No alcohol. Works as a graphic designer. Lives with partner. No children.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Thin female appearing chronically ill**; **Mild distress**. HEENT: **Pale conjunctivae**; **Dry mucous membranes**. Neck: Supple. Cardiovascular: **Regular, mildly tachycardic**; No murmurs. Pulmonary: Clear bilaterally. Abdomen: **Midline infraumbilical incision — superior portion is well-healed**; **The inferior 3 cm of the incision is erythematous, indurated, and has a 1 cm opening from which greenish-brown, feculent-appearing fluid is actively draining**; **The surrounding skin is macerated and excoriated**; **RLQ tenderness with a palpable fullness/fluctuance deep to the incision**; **Mild distension**; No frank peritoneal signs; Bowel sounds present; No hepatosplenomegaly; **Perianal: Healed scar from prior seton**; No active fistula or abscess; No fissures. Rectal: **Guaiac positive**. Extremities: No edema; **Erythema nodosum noted on bilateral shins (2-3 tender, red nodules)**. Skin: **Excoriated skin surrounding the fistula opening**; **Thin, fragile skin**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Worsening anemia (baseline 10.8 pre-op), from chronic disease, iron deficiency, and now ongoing inflammatory state
Significantly low, reflecting malnutrition, active inflammation, and protein loss
Markedly elevated, confirms active inflammatory/infectious process

lab_results.result: Greenish-brown fluid, pH 7.8, bilirubin positive, amylase elevated at 2,400 U/L
lab_results.clinical_significance: Enteric fistula output confirmed by alkaline pH and elevated amylase/bilirubin content. High amylase suggests small bowel origin (proximal).

lab_results.result: Mixed enteric flora: E. coli, Enterococcus faecalis, Bacteroides fragilis
lab_results.clinical_significance: Polymicrobial enteric organisms confirm the fistula communicates with the bowel lumen

imaging_results.test_name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Oral and IV Contrast
imaging_results.result: A 5.2 x 3.8 cm rim-enhancing fluid collection in the right lower quadrant adjacent to the ileocolic anastomosis, consistent with an abscess. A tract is seen extending from the abscess to the anterior abdominal wall at the inferior aspect of the surgical incision, confirming an enterocutaneous fistula. Oral contrast is seen within the abscess cavity and fistula tract. The ileocolic anastomosis appears disrupted at its inferior aspect. Mild thickening of the neo-terminal ileum proximal to the anastomosis (possible recurrent this condition). No other intra-abdominal collections. No small bowel obstruction.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: CT confirms the diagnosis of postoperative enterocutaneous fistula with an associated intra-abdominal abscess secondary to partial anastomotic dehiscence at the ileocolic anastomosis. Recurrent this condition at the anastomotic site may be contributing.

imaging_results.test_name: Fistulogram (contrast injection through the fistula opening)
imaging_results.result: Water-soluble contrast injected through the cutaneous opening opacifies a tract leading to a 5 cm abscess cavity, which communicates with the bowel lumen at the ileocolic anastomosis. A single tract is identified. No other fistula connections.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Fistulogram maps the anatomy of the fistula: a single tract from the anastomosis through the abscess to the skin. This information is critical for surgical planning if operative management is pursued.

acceptable_tests: Small bowel follow-through or MR enterography (assess for recurrent Crohn disease)
low_yield_tests: Barium enema (avoid barium with fistulae — use water-soluble contrast)
stage3.final_clue: CT reveals a 5.2 cm abscess adjacent to the ileocolic anastomosis with a fistula tract to the skin. Feculent drainage from the incision, wound culture growing enteric flora, and fistula output analysis confirm an enterocutaneous fistula. This is a recognized surgical complication of Crohn disease, likely secondary to partial anastomotic dehiscence in the setting of active disease at the anastomotic site. Management requires abscess drainage, nutritional optimization, and a stepwise approach before considering reoperation.

stage5.final_clue: CT reveals a 5.2 cm abscess adjacent to the ileocolic anastomosis with a fistula tract to the skin.

debrief.classic_presentation: Fever, abdominal pain, and enteric drainage from a surgical wound or drain site occurring weeks after bowel resection in a Crohn disease patient. Enterocutaneous fistulae (ECF) occur in 5-20% of patients following surgery for Crohn disease, particularly at the anastomotic site. Crohn disease is the most common cause of ECF overall. The drainage is typically feculent or bilious depending on the level of origin. The volume of output determines classification: low-output (<200 mL/day), moderate (200-500 mL/day), and high-output (>500 mL/day), which guides fluid and electrolyte management.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: This is a complex surgical complication case with multiple attending-level management decisions: (1) The initial management follows the mnemonic SNAP: Sepsis control (drain the abscess — percutaneous CT-guided drainage is preferred initially over surgery), Nutritional optimization (this patient's albumin of 2.4 and prealbumin of 9 mandate aggressive nutritional support — TPN is usually needed, as it also reduces fistula output by putting the bowel at rest), Anatomy definition (fistulogram to map the tract, identify the source, and look for distal obstruction — a fistula will NOT heal if there is distal obstruction or undrained abscess), and Planning for definitive surgery. (2) Factors predicting fistula closure without surgery (FRIEND mnemonic of factors that PREVENT closure): Foreign body, Radiation, Infection/IBD, Epithelialization of the tract, Neoplasm, Distal obstruction. This patient has IBD (Crohn disease), making spontaneous closure unlikely. (3) Timing of reoperation: Do NOT rush to reoperate. The patient needs 6-12 weeks of nutritional optimization (goal albumin >3.0), sepsis control, and medical treatment of Crohn disease before reoperation. Operating on a malnourished, septic patient with active Crohn disease will lead to further complications and fistula recurrence. (4) Medical therapy for Crohn disease: The patient's azathioprine is held and infliximab caused reactions. Consider vedolizumab (gut-selective anti-integrin, no prior use) or ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23). Starting biologic therapy before and after surgery improves outcomes. (5) Skin care around the fistula is critical: the enteric contents cause severe skin breakdown. Apply stoma pouching system or wound manager over the fistula opening. Consult wound/ostomy nurse. (6) At reoperation: resect the fistulized segment and anastomose in healthy bowel. Ensure no distal obstruction. Consider temporary diverting loop ileostomy to protect the new anastomosis.
debrief.pathophysiology: Enterocutaneous fistulae in Crohn disease develop through several mechanisms: (1) Anastomotic dehiscence: Crohn disease impairs wound healing due to chronic inflammation, malnutrition, corticosteroid use, and active disease at the anastomotic margins. Recurrent Crohn disease at the anastomosis is common (endoscopic recurrence in 70-90% within 1 year). (2) The transmural nature of Crohn inflammation predisposes to fistula formation — inflammation penetrates all bowel wall layers and can track to adjacent organs or the skin. (3) The fistula tract matures through a sequence: initial leak, abscess formation (walled off by omentum and adjacent structures), then drainage to the path of least resistance (often the surgical wound). (4) Factors that prevent spontaneous fistula closure include ongoing active Crohn disease, distal bowel obstruction (high-pressure proximal to the fistula), undrained abscess, epithelialization of the fistula tract, and malnutrition.
debrief.treatment_overview: Phase 1 — Stabilization (weeks 1-2): Sepsis control with abscess drainage (CT-guided percutaneous drain if accessible, or surgical drainage if percutaneous not feasible). Broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem). Fluid and electrolyte replacement (particularly important for high-output fistulae). Fistula output management (stoma pouch, wound manager). Skin protection. Phase 2 — Nutritional Optimization (weeks 2-8): TPN to provide adequate calories (25-30 kcal/kg/day) and protein (1.5-2 g/kg/day). Bowel rest (NPO or minimal enteral nutrition) to reduce fistula output. Goal: albumin >3.0, prealbumin >15, BMI >18.5. Micronutrient repletion (iron, B12, zinc, vitamin C, vitamin D). Phase 3 — Crohn Disease Medical Therapy: Start/optimize biologic therapy (vedolizumab or ustekinumab given infliximab allergy). Restart immunomodulator (azathioprine) if appropriate. Smoking cessation reinforcement. Phase 4 — Definitive Surgery (after 6-12 weeks): Resection of fistulized segment with reanastomosis in healthy, well-vascularized bowel. Consider protective loop ileostomy. Ensure no distal obstruction. Up to 30-40% of ECFs in Crohn patients may close with non-operative management, but the presence of active Crohn disease makes spontaneous closure less likely.
high_yield_pearls: The SNAP approach guides ECF management: Sepsis control, Nutrition optimization, Anatomy definition (fistulogram), and Planned surgery.
Factors preventing spontaneous fistula closure (FRIEND): Foreign body, Radiation, Infection/IBD, Epithelialization, Neoplasm, Distal obstruction.
High-output fistulae (>500 mL/day) cause significant fluid and electrolyte derangements (dehydration, hyponatremia, hypokalemia, metabolic acidosis) and require aggressive IV replacement.
Operative intervention for ECF should be delayed 6-12 weeks to allow nutritional optimization and resolution of the 'hostile abdomen' from the initial surgery.
Crohn disease is the most common cause of enterocutaneous fistulae, and recurrence at the anastomosis is the most common site of postoperative recurrence.
Smoking is the single most important modifiable risk factor for Crohn disease recurrence after surgery — cessation must be reinforced.
sources: Gribovskaja-Rupp I, Melton GB. Clin Colon Rectal Surg 2016;29:138-149 (Enterocutaneous Fistula) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
Schwartz's Principles of Surgery, 11th Edition, Ch. 28 — https://accessmedicine.mhmedical.com/book.aspx?bookID=2576
Fazio VW et al. Ann Surg 2013;257:108-116 (Crohn Disease Recurrence After Ileocolic Resection) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/
Schecter WP et al. J Am Coll Surg 2009;209:484-491 (Management of Enterocutaneous Fistulae) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/

reasonable_ddx: Enterocutaneous fistula with abscess (Crohn disease complication)
